title: Asses To Ashes

jokes.text: What, a Pizza Hut in the garage?
jokes.setup: Carlton says he'll get his dad what he always wanted
jokes.punchline: Will's ridiculous suggestion of a Pizza Hut in the garage
jokes.explanation: Sharp, unexpected response that fits Will's streetwise perspective contrasting with Carlton's earnestness. Good economy and character voice.

jokes.text: Hello, is President Bush there?
jokes.setup: Carlton wants to get an influential endorsement
jokes.punchline: He naively calls the White House expecting to reach the President directly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton moment - his privileged naivety makes this completely believable for his character while being absurd to the audience.

jokes.text: Carlton, are you by any chance an idiot?
jokes.setup: Carlton's delusional phone call to the President
jokes.punchline: Will's perfectly timed, deadpan question
jokes.explanation: Classic Will straight-man response. The understatement and timing make it funnier than a direct insult would be.

jokes.text: I'm in a position to scratch his back if he'll scratch mine, capische?
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to sound like a political insider
jokes.punchline: Using 'capische' while being completely powerless
jokes.explanation: Carlton's pretentious language mixed with his complete lack of actual influence creates the comedy.

jokes.text: I've been saving that genteel pleasure to calm myself lest I get too giddy from scrubbing the john.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey being asked about getting the mail
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic, overly formal response about mundane tasks
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Geoffrey moment - his refined vocabulary applied to menial tasks creates perfect class-conscious humor. The contrast is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: You know, you really need a woman, G. / What's a woman?
jokes.setup: Will suggests Geoffrey needs a woman
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's deadpan 'What's a woman?' response
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup-punchline structure with maximum economy. Geoffrey's deadpan delivery makes it land harder.

jokes.text: All right, Hilary, how much do you want? / No, I'm talking about... Well, $100.
jokes.setup: Hilary starts talking about loving her father and the election
jokes.punchline: Philip immediately assumes she wants money, and she admits she does
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection that establishes both characters perfectly - Philip's cynicism about his daughter and Hilary's materialism.

jokes.text: where do you stand on a woman's right to choose the restaurant? / I think we should leave that up to the Supreme Court.
jokes.setup: Hilary asking about women's rights issues
jokes.punchline: Twisting 'right to choose' to be about restaurant selection, Philip's serious political response
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on a serious political issue with Philip's oblivious serious response. The absurdity of referring restaurant choice to the Supreme Court is brilliant.

jokes.text: Self magazine... Shape magazine... and Sassy magazine. And for you, sir, Elf magazine... Ape magazine... and Sissy magazine.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey reading magazine names for Hilary
jokes.punchline: Transforming them into insulting variations for Philip
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay escalation. The systematic transformation of magazine names shows real craft, and it's a perfect Geoffrey move - subtle rebellion through wordplay.

jokes.text: But Dad, aren't some of those lies? / Carlton, honey, they're all lies.
jokes.setup: Carlton's innocent question about the attack ad
jokes.punchline: Vivian's matter-of-fact correction
jokes.explanation: Great character contrast - Carlton's naivety vs Vivian's blunt realism about politics.

jokes.text: Uncle Phil, let me tell you a little story. / Oh, no.
jokes.setup: Will about to tell one of his stories
jokes.punchline: Philip's immediate dread reaction
jokes.explanation: Classic setup for Will's stories with Philip's predictable but effective reaction. Works because it's established character dynamics.

jokes.text: Amen, sister.
jokes.setup: Will describing loving someone with his whole heart and soul
jokes.punchline: Breaking character to agree with his own romantic description
jokes.explanation: Will breaking his own narrative flow to comment on his story. Good character moment but fairly simple.

jokes.text: So, this story is about you, isn't it, Will? / That's right, Uncle Phil. And to this day I just thank God I had my two-headed coin... 'cause that girl was smoking, man.
jokes.setup: Philip thinks Will is the nice guy in the story
jokes.punchline: Will reveals he was the cheater with the two-headed coin
jokes.explanation: Masterful misdirection. The entire story setup makes the audience assume Will is the victim, then he completely subverts it. Perfect Will Smith delivery and character work.

jokes.text: If Banks gets into office, we won't have to hide anymore... this city's ours!
jokes.setup: Political attack ad claiming criminals support Philip Banks
jokes.punchline: Criminals in the ad accidentally proving the point by being obviously criminal
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing hilariously obvious 'criminals' in the attack ad, undermining its own message through poor casting choices.

jokes.text: He like begging, he like, 'Philip Banks, kick my butt.' / Philip Banks, kick the man's butt.
jokes.setup: Will trying to convince Uncle Phil to fight back
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic, street-style encouragement
jokes.explanation: Pure Will energy and speech patterns applied to political strategy. Good character work but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: Because it goes against everything I stand for. / What's your point?
jokes.setup: Phil explaining why he won't fight dirty
jokes.punchline: Vivian's blunt response suggesting his principles don't matter
jokes.explanation: Sharp character moment - Vivian's pragmatism cutting through Phil's idealism with perfect timing.

jokes.text: I have faith in L.A. That L.A. will have faith in me. / ...Judge Robertson has overwhelmingly defeated Philip Banks. / I hate L.A.
jokes.setup: Philip expressing faith in Los Angeles voters
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to his crushing defeat and bitter reversal
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing and structure. The immediate reversal from faith to hate after the election results is classically well-executed.

jokes.text: Well, once when I was a kid I zipped my pants up too fast... let's just say this brings back old memories.
jokes.setup: Reporter asking how it feels to lose so badly
jokes.punchline: Philip's self-deprecating comparison to a childhood zipper accident
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly personal and relatable metaphor for political humiliation. Good structure and universal relatability.

jokes.text: So tell me, are you free for dinner tonight?
jokes.setup: Reporter noting Phil's humiliating defeat and introducing Hilary
jokes.punchline: Immediately hitting on her at the worst possible moment
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor - inappropriate timing makes it funnier. Professional becoming personal.

jokes.text: Look, my father just lost an election. I mean, I'm not totally insensitive. / How about tomorrow? / Great.
jokes.setup: Reporter asking Hilary out right after her father's defeat
jokes.punchline: Hilary showing 'sensitivity' by waiting until tomorrow
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hilary moment - her shallow definition of sensitivity while being completely self-centered. Great character integration.

jokes.text: Damn! Uncle Phil, I got your back. Well, I got like half your back 'cause your back's like...
jokes.setup: Will trying to be supportive of Uncle Phil
jokes.punchline: Visual gag about Uncle Phil's size preventing full back coverage
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy about Uncle Phil's size. Will's attempt at support undercut by practical considerations. Good visual setup.

jokes.text: Carl... / I thought I was Carl. / I'm Philip. / And I am the walrus.
jokes.setup: Judge Robertson getting names confused
jokes.punchline: His completely nonsensical Beatles reference
jokes.explanation: Perfectly demonstrates Robertson's senility through absurd Beatles reference. The randomness is earned by character setup.

jokes.text: There goes Philip Banks... the biggest sap that ever lived. What a stooge.
jokes.setup: Robertson appearing confused and harmless
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's actually quite aware and cruel
jokes.explanation: Good character revelation that he's not as senile as he appears. Creates dramatic irony.

jokes.text: Really? And what would that be? A buffet?
jokes.setup: Will saying his uncle stands for something
jokes.punchline: Robertson's fat joke about Philip
jokes.explanation: Predictable fat joke that serves the character but lacks originality. Gets the job done but relies on cheap humor.

jokes.text: And I'll tell you another thing, if you don't like it, man, you can drop dead.
jokes.setup: Will defending Uncle Phil with increasing passion
jokes.punchline: His unfortunate choice of words right before Robertson dies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant setup for the episode's central premise. The irony is perfectly structured and becomes the engine driving the rest of the story.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, he's dead. / Come on, man, if I told you to jump off a bridge, would you do it?
jokes.setup: Robertson has just died after Will told him to drop dead
jokes.punchline: Will's immediate attempt to deflect responsibility with playground logic
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with Will's childish logic applied to a death. The callback structure works perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, he's dead. / Come on, man, if I told you to jump off a bridge, would you do it?
jokes.setup: Robertson has just died from Will's words
jokes.punchline: Will treating it like Robertson was just being overly obedient
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - treating death as following orders too literally. Classic Will deflection through humor.

jokes.text: Well, Will, you really knocked them dead the other night, huh? / Don't get him mad, Mom. You don't want to make him raise his voice.
jokes.setup: Family treating Will like he has deadly powers
jokes.punchline: Carlton's fearful warning about Will's voice
jokes.explanation: Good callback humor with the family's superstitious fear of Will's words. Carlton's specific warning escalates the absurdity.

jokes.text: Don't get him mad, Mom. You don't want to make him raise his voice.
jokes.setup: Family discussing Will's 'power'
jokes.punchline: Carlton treating Will's voice as literally deadly
jokes.explanation: Good callback humor extending the death joke. Carlton's fearful reverence is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: I did it last week, it's the Terminator's turn.
jokes.setup: Carlton being asked to take out trash
jokes.punchline: Referring to Will as 'the Terminator' due to perceived deadly powers
jokes.explanation: Solid callback with pop culture reference that fits Carlton's character. Good economy of words.

jokes.text: Simple. Thanks to the Grim Reaper over here... and Judge Robertson taking the old dirt nap...
jokes.setup: Carlton explaining his Dan Quayle call
jokes.punchline: Calling Will the Grim Reaper and using euphemism for death
jokes.explanation: Carlton's formal language applied to death humor. 'Dirt nap' euphemism works well.

jokes.text: The phone's dead, Will.
jokes.setup: Carlton trying to call Dan Quayle when phone stops working
jokes.punchline: Blaming Will for 'killing' the phone too
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - just three words that perfectly capture the running gag. The double meaning of 'dead' works well.

jokes.text: Look, I think I know how to help Will. This calls for tough love. We've gotta draw him out. We've gotta bait him, confront him... attack him until he defends himself.
jokes.setup: Carlton proposing a psychological solution
jokes.punchline: His plan is to literally attack Will, the opposite of helping
jokes.explanation: Carlton's naive psychology mixed with potentially dangerous advice. Good character moment showing his book-smart but life-dumb nature.

jokes.text: Look, I think I know how to help Will. This calls for tough love.
jokes.setup: Will's depression about the incident
jokes.punchline: Carlton's clinical approach to emotional problems
jokes.explanation: Classic Carlton - treating psychology like a textbook. Sets up his misguided intervention.

jokes.text: Will, you should be ashamed of yourself. How could you do such a thing? Hope you never get mad at me.
jokes.setup: Carlton's plan to confront Will to help him
jokes.punchline: Hilary's aside showing she understands the danger
jokes.explanation: Good escalation where Hilary breaks character to acknowledge the danger Carlton is ignoring.

jokes.text: Why are y'all screaming at me? I was minding my damn business eating some fruit.
jokes.setup: Family 'attacking' Will per Carlton's plan
jokes.punchline: Will's confused, defensive response about just eating fruit
jokes.explanation: Will's genuine confusion and the mundane detail of eating fruit makes his anger funnier. Good escalation moment.

jokes.text: Well, I could be wrong.
jokes.setup: Carlton's therapy plan exploding in his face
jokes.punchline: His understated admission of failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton response - clinical understatement after complete failure. Great economy of words.

jokes.text: You won't believe this. They want me to give the eulogy at Judge Robertson's funeral. / Philip, you know, I really hate to speak ill of the dead... but if that little man hadn't died I would have killed him myself.
jokes.setup: Philip being asked to eulogize his opponent
jokes.punchline: Vivian's contradictory statement about not speaking ill while threatening murder
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect contradiction - claiming not to speak ill while immediately speaking ill. Vivian's rarely-seen vindictive side.

jokes.text: Philip, you know, I really hate to speak ill of the dead... but if that little man hadn't died I would have killed him myself.
jokes.setup: Discussing giving eulogy for Robertson
jokes.punchline: Vivian's polite introduction followed by violent sentiment
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of polite funeral talk. Vivian's refined manner makes the violent sentiment funnier.

jokes.text: Philip, any chance of getting those flowers after the funeral? / Sit down, Vivian.
jokes.setup: At Robertson's funeral
jokes.punchline: Vivian asking about taking the funeral flowers
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor with Vivian's inappropriate practicality at a funeral. Philip's mortified reaction sells it.

jokes.text: He was a judge. A judge named Carl. But he was more. So much more. Carl... He wasn't a very heavy man.
jokes.setup: Philip trying to give a eulogy without his prepared speech
jokes.punchline: His increasingly desperate and meaningless observations
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe comedy as Philip struggles with having nothing positive to say. The weight comment is desperately random.

jokes.text: The jerk sentenced me to six months in jail for a crime I didn't commit. I'm just here to make sure he dead.
jokes.setup: Phil asking for warm memories of Robertson
jokes.punchline: Attendee's brutal honesty about checking if he's really dead
jokes.explanation: Dark but honest humor. The blunt admission of coming to confirm death is brutally funny.

jokes.text: The jerk sentenced me to six months in jail for a crime I didn't commit. I'm just here to make sure he dead.
jokes.setup: Philip asking for someone with warm memories of Robertson
jokes.punchline: Attendee sharing hatred and checking if he's really dead
jokes.explanation: Dark funeral humor with the brutal honesty about just wanting to confirm death. Good subversion of funeral expectations.

jokes.text: With him it was just a bunch of... sex all the time, like dating Thumper.
jokes.setup: Mistress describing relationship with Robertson
jokes.punchline: Comparing him to the rabbit from Bambi in a sexual context
jokes.explanation: Inappropriate funeral content with unexpected Disney reference. The juxtaposition is what makes it work.

jokes.text: When you gonna bring your sister around?
jokes.setup: Asked about Robertson's idea of sweet talk
jokes.punchline: His crude request about her sister
jokes.explanation: Efficiently demonstrates Robertson's sleaziness through reported dialogue. Good economy and character building.

jokes.text: The man's idea of sweet talk was: 'When you gonna bring your sister around?'
jokes.setup: Woman describing Robertson's romantic deficiencies
jokes.punchline: His 'sweet talk' involving her sister
jokes.explanation: Great specific example of Robertson's awfulness. The quote format makes it very funny and quotable.

jokes.text: I'm the dude that killed him.
jokes.setup: Will giving passionate speech about Robertson's humanity
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact admission to causing the death
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and punchline. The contrast between Will's noble speech and casual admission is brilliantly structured. Maximum comedic impact.

jokes.text: And hell is like the Valley.
jokes.setup: Hilary describing her vision of heaven and hell
jokes.punchline: Dismissing the San Fernando Valley as hell
jokes.explanation: Classic LA snobbishness from Hilary. Perfect character moment with geographic humor that plays to the setting.

jokes.text: I think we're all damn lucky Hilary has a job.
jokes.setup: Hilary's shallow description of heaven and hell
jokes.punchline: Phil's dry assessment of his daughter's intellect
jokes.explanation: Good parental exasperation humor. Phil's deadpan acknowledgment of Hilary's limitations is affectionately brutal.

jokes.text: What do you think, Daddy? / I think we're all damn lucky Hilary has a job. / Thanks, Daddy.
jokes.setup: Hilary sharing her philosophical thoughts
jokes.punchline: Philip's backhanded gratitude for her employment
jokes.explanation: Perfect father-daughter dynamic with Philip's diplomatic way of calling Hilary an airhead. Her oblivious thanks makes it better.

jokes.text: I think we're all damn lucky Hilary has a job.
jokes.setup: Hilary's shallow description of heaven and hell
jokes.punchline: Phil's dry assessment of her intelligence
jokes.explanation: Classic Phil deadpan response to Hilary's airheadedness. The family dynamic works perfectly.

jokes.text: See, it's like I can have a leg in one hand and a breast in the other.
jokes.setup: Will describing his vision of heaven
jokes.punchline: Innocent description of chicken that sounds sexual
jokes.explanation: Double entendre humor that's more obvious than clever. Works for character but relies on cheap sexual innuendo.

jokes.text: See, it's like I can have a leg in one hand and a breast in the other.
jokes.setup: Will describing his version of heaven
jokes.punchline: Chicken and waffles description that sounds sexual
jokes.explanation: Double entendre humor with chicken parts. Works as both innocent food talk and suggestive humor.

jokes.text: I think I must have been very bad in a previous life.
jokes.setup: Being asked about his view of afterlife while serving this family
jokes.punchline: His deadpan explanation for his current situation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Geoffrey line that perfectly captures his suffering while maintaining his dignified demeanor. Excellent character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Why are y'all screaming at me? I was minding my damn business eating some fruit. / You screamed at me.
jokes.setup: Repetition of earlier fruit-eating line
jokes.punchline: Will's continued confusion about being blamed
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier moment but doesn't add much new. Functional but not particularly clever on its own.

jokes.text: Why are y'all screaming at me? I was minding my damn business eating some fruit. You screamed at me.
jokes.setup: Episode callback to earlier scene
jokes.punchline: Will's repeated indignant fruit-eating defense
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reinforces Will's character and provides episode symmetry. The repetition works well.

jokes.text: Well, I could be wrong. / What in the hell did you just do with your lips?
jokes.setup: Carlton's repeated understated admission
jokes.punchline: Will's reaction to some visual comedy from Carlton
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy we can only infer from Will's reaction. The callback to Carlton's line provides structure.

tmdb_overview: Election results are in and Philip fights to remain cool amid lies by Judge Robertson, which prompt Will to confront the outspoken incumbent. Will Will go to far? Will he say what everyone is feeling? Will Judge Robertson be able to take it?
